food_subgroup: Deep-fried milk solids soaked in rose sugar syrup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Gulab Jamun. Nearest parent is a fried dairy confectionery in syrup. A new concept for Indian milk-based syrup desserts is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried milk solid confection in sugar syrup, Indian (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Gulab Jamun; A14AS used as closest parent for fried confectionery in syrup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Gulab Jamun as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Gulab Jamun is 2 pieces (approximately 100 g), providing around 342 kcal, 48.5 g of sugar, and 12.5 g of fat.

nutrient_source.title: Gulab Jamun — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F11100008; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database food code F11100008 for Gulab Jamun, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables for micronutrient validation. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Indian Dairy-Based Fried Confections in Sugar Syrup — estimated from closest analogous food in University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Gulab Jamun exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 (high) is estimated from the closest analogous food: dairy-based fried confection soaked in refined sugar syrup. The high GI is consistent with the very high refined sugar content (48.5 g/100 g from sugar syrup) and use of refined flour (maida). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (76 × 55.5) / 100 = 42.2, confirming very high glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (88 mg/100 g); primary health concerns are sugar and fat, not sodium
hypertension.guidance: Gulab Jamun is low in sodium (88 mg/100 g) and does not pose a direct hypertensive risk from salt; however, its high sugar and saturated fat content (6.8 g/100 g) may contribute to weight gain and metabolic dysfunction that indirectly elevates blood pressure, so consumption should remain occasional.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (76) and very high sugar content (48.5 g/100 g); glycemic load of 42.2 per 100 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gulab Jamun must be avoided or strictly limited by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes — a single 100 g serving (2 pieces) delivers a glycemic load of approximately 42, driven by 48.5 g of rapidly absorbed sugar from the syrup and refined flour, causing sharp postprandial blood glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (6.8 g/100 g) from khoya and frying oil; high sugar content promoting triglyceride elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Gulab Jamun to rare festive occasions, as the combination of 6.8 g saturated fat and 48.5 g sugar per 100 g can raise LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, increasing atherogenic risk with regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sugar content (48.5 g/100 g) promotes inflammatory cytokine production; deep-frying adds pro-inflammatory fats
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Gulab Jamun should be consumed very sparingly by patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the high sugar content and deep-frying combination are strongly pro-inflammatory; limit to 1 piece on rare festive occasions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gulab Jamun is one of India's most iconic and beloved desserts — small, round balls made from khoya (reduced evaporated milk solids, also called mawa) mixed with a small amount of all-purpose flour (maida) and a leavening agent, deep-fried in ghee or oil until uniformly dark golden-brown, then immediately immersed in warm sugar syrup flavoured with rosewater and green cardamom until they absorb the syrup and become plump, soft, and glistening. The texture is uniquely spongy and melt-in-the-mouth; the flavour is intensely sweet and milky with a floral rose note and a hint of cardamom warmth. Per 100 g, Gulab Jamun provides 342 kcal, 5.5 g protein, 12.5 g fat (6.8 g saturated), 55.5 g carbohydrate, and 48.5 g sugar — making it one of the highest-sugar Indian desserts. It is a meaningful source of calcium (125 mg/100 g) from the khoya. The glycemic index is approximately 76 (high), with a glycemic load of ~42 per 100 g serving, making it unsuitable for diabetic patients. It is a centrepiece of Indian celebrations, weddings, and festivals such as Diwali and Eid, and is widely served at Indian restaurants and sweet shops in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (food code F11100008) — an approved primary source for Indian dishes served in Singapore — cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food with documented basis. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Gulab Jamun not separately reported in HPB Foodo Database F11100008 or ICMR-NIN tables as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food code. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo Database F11100008. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; Gulab Jamun contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients. GI value of 76 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (closest match: dairy-based fried confection in refined sugar syrup); no direct GI measurement for Gulab Jamun was found in the database. Copper (0.05 mg), manganese (0.02 mg), and vitamin K (0.8 mcg) values sourced from enriched_json cross-reference with confidence 0.9; these micronutrients are not separately reported in the primary HPB source. Portions cleaned: duplicate '1 serving (100g)' entries consolidated; canonical portions are 1 piece (50 g), 2 pieces (100 g), and 3 pieces (150 g).
